book_claims.claim: Used in multiple Longevity Diet dishes. Maitake mushrooms are the top food source of vitamin D among mushrooms (786 IU per cup raw, 196.5% DV), the highest of any non-animal food when UV-exposed.
book_claims.mechanism: Mushrooms synthesize vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) upon UV light exposure, analogous to human skin producing vitamin D3; maitake's ergosterol-rich structure makes it the most potent dietary vitamin D source among mushrooms.

supplementary_research.finding: Maitake mushrooms contain beta-glucan polysaccharides (D-fraction) that stimulate NK cell and macrophage activity, demonstrating significant immunomodulatory effects in human cancer patients and healthy volunteers. (PMID 23402898)

Maitake D-fraction (beta-glucan extract) inhibits tumor growth in mouse models and enhances the efficacy of standard chemotherapy; supports inclusion of maitake in cancer-preventive longevity diets. (PMID 19193149)

UV-exposed maitake mushrooms (placed gills-up in sunlight for 1–2 hours) generate sufficient vitamin D2 to meet or exceed daily requirements; equivalent to 9 mcg (360 IU) per gram dry weight after sun exposure. (PMID 25866155)

Maitake beta-glucans activate innate immune pathways (TLR2, Dectin-1 receptors), reduce blood glucose, and demonstrate anti-tumor activity; vitamin D2 from sun-exposed maitake is bioavailable and raises serum 25(OH)D2 comparably to supplemental vitamin D2.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: UV-exposed maitake is the richest plant-kingdom vitamin D source; cooking reduces content by ~50% — use raw or lightly sautéed; gills-up sun exposure for 30–60 min maximizes D2
Partially digested; activate pattern recognition receptors on immune cells; not destroyed by cooking
Unique thiol antioxidant synthesized only by fungi; stable to cooking; accumulates in high-stress tissues (liver, kidney, brain)
Mushrooms are good copper sources; essential for immune cell function and antioxidant enzymes

synergies.description: Fat in olive oil significantly enhances absorption of fat-soluble vitamin D2 from maitake — always sauté maitake in olive oil or dress with olive oil
Herring provides vitamin D3 while maitake provides D2; together they cover both major dietary forms of vitamin D, supporting more complete vitamin D status
Classic culinary pairing in Italian longevity cooking; garlic's allicin and maitake's beta-glucans provide complementary immunomodulatory effects
Brightness of lemon contrasts maitake's earthiness; vitamin C in lemon may enhance uptake of non-heme iron present in the mushroom
